one_line_takeaway: AJAR reframes multi-turn LLM jailbreak attacks (Crescendo, ActorAttack, X-Teaming) as callable MCP services orchestrated by an Auditor Agent with explicit rollback/tool-simulation control, showing on 200 HarmBench behaviors that this context control raises ASR over native implementations and PyRIT, and that tool access non-monotonically reshapes (rather than uniformly amplifies) which attacks and categories succeed.
key_finding: On the 200 HarmBench validation behaviors: (1) AJAR improves X-Teaming ASR from 65.0% (baseline, 95% CI [58.0, 71.5]) to 76.0% (AJAR, 95% CI [70.0, 82.0]), a +11.0 point gain significant at p<0.001; AJAR reaches 80% cumulative success after 5 turns vs. 6 for the native implementation; paired analysis shows AJAR uniquely succeeds on 47 behaviors vs. 25 uniquely solved by the baseline (net +22), and 36 of the 47 AJAR-only successes involved at least one rollback_conversation event, with gains concentrated in illegal behaviors (63.6% of baseline failures recovered), chemical/biological (73.3% recovered), and cybercrime/intrusion (75.0% recovered); misinformation/disinformation is the one category where the baseline still holds an edge (85.3% vs. 76.5%). (2) AJAR reproduces Crescendo more effectively than PyRIT: 91.0% ASR vs. 87.5% for PyRIT, with AJAR converging faster (mean 3.42 turns to success vs. 3.80 for PyRIT); AJAR uniquely succeeds on 22 behaviors PyRIT misses (rescuing 22 of PyRIT's 25 failures) vs. 15 uniquely solved by PyRIT; on the 160 jointly-successful behaviors AJAR finishes earlier in 72 cases vs. 46 for PyRIT (42 ties). (3) Tool access has non-monotonic, attack-dependent effects: ActorAttack rises from 51.0% (AJAR, text-only) to 56.0% ASR with tools (net +10 behaviors: 42 gained, 32 lost), while Crescendo falls from 91.0% to 78.0% (net -26: 11 gained, 37 lost) and X-Teaming falls from 76.0% to 55.5% (net -41: 23 gained, 64 lost). Category-level breakdowns show the sharpest tool-induced declines in categories relying on long semantic buildup (e.g. X-Teaming: general harmful behaviors -33.3 points, illegal behaviors -32.8, chemical/biological -25.0), whereas ActorAttack, which depends more on front-loaded role planning, gains in several categories (general harmful +28.6, chemical/biological +17.9) while losing on misinformation/disinformation (-14.7).
core_contribution: AJAR (Adaptive Jailbreak Architecture for Red-teaming) is an orchestration layer that exposes multi-turn jailbreak attack algorithms (Crescendo, ActorAttack, X-Teaming) as callable MCP (Model Context Protocol) services, decoupled from an Auditor Agent that separately controls the target-visible conversation state (rollback, branch pruning, system-message injection) and a simulated tool environment, all built on top of the Petri agent runtime (which itself sits on Inspect AI). The key architectural idea is separating 'strategy generation' (the attack-specific planning/scoring logic, exposed as a stable service interface: initialize/generate_question/evaluate for Crescendo, generate_actor/get_next_question/evaluate for ActorAttack, generate_plan/optimize_question/revise_plan/evaluate for X-Teaming) from 'runtime control' (the Auditor's decisions to commit, rollback, branch, or modify the simulated tool environment). This lets heterogeneous attack algorithms, originally packaged as monolithic scripts, be transplanted into an agentic evaluation loop with shared primitives such as conversation rollback and synthetic tool injection, without collapsing them into a single canonical attack loop.
threat_model: The paper's own threat model is an automated red-teamer (the Auditor Agent, backed by DeepSeek V3.2) attacking a target LLM (Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, deterministic decoding) with the goal of eliciting harmful compliance on HarmBench-style behaviors (e.g. weapons instructions, hate mail, extraction of toxic compounds) across up to 7 dialogue turns with up to 3 retries per turn. The paper explicitly reframes the threat surface as moving from 'content moderation' (does the model emit harmful text) to 'action security' (can adaptive multi-turn dialogue plus tool access get an agentic system to translate unsafe intent into external actions). Success is measured with a 5-point compliance rubric (Score 1 = explicit refusal, Scores 4-5 = successful jailbreak = ASR), using each attack family's own native scoring prompt to preserve fidelity rather than forcing one unified judge across all three attacks. A second, tool-augmented condition adds a simulated tool environment (including 'honeypot' blocked high-risk tools) to study whether tool access changes attacker success, without allowing real-world side effects.
limitations: The paper itself flags: (a) the tool-access comparison in Table 2 mixes attacks scored with different native success-scoring prompts (Crescendo differs from ActorAttack/X-Teaming), so absolute cross-attack ranking is explicitly discouraged by the authors, only within-attack comparisons across settings; (b) the target model is a single fixed model (Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct) and the Auditor is a single fixed model (DeepSeek V3.2), so generalization to other target/auditor model pairs is untested; (c) the tool environment is simulated rather than a real agentic deployment with genuine side effects, which the authors note is deliberate (ethics/safety) but means the 'action security' claims are evaluated in a controlled proxy setting rather than a live agent harness; (d) attacks are capped at 7 turns with up to 3 retries per turn, a fixed budget that could interact with the observed turn-to-success differences; (e) the paper describes results as coming from 'the thesis experiments that motivate this paper,' suggesting this arXiv preprint condenses a larger underlying study whose full experimental detail is not reproduced here.

quantitative_results: AJAR reaches 80% cumulative success at turn 5 vs turn 6 for native X-Teaming
Paired X-Teaming outcomes: AJAR-only successes 47, baseline-only successes 25, both succeed 105, both fail 23 (net +22 for AJAR)
36 of 47 AJAR-only X-Teaming successes involved at least one rollback_conversation event
AJAR recovers 63.6% of baseline's illegal-behavior failures, 73.3% of chemical/biological failures, 75.0% of cybercrime failures
Misinformation/disinformation: baseline 85.3% vs AJAR 76.5% (baseline retains edge)
AJAR vs PyRIT Crescendo ASR: 91.0% vs 87.5%
Mean turns to successful Crescendo jailbreak: AJAR 3.42 vs PyRIT 3.80
Paired Crescendo outcomes: AJAR-only successes 22, PyRIT-only successes 15, both succeed on 160 behaviors (AJAR finishes earlier in 72, PyRIT in 46, tie in 42)

Crescendo/general-harmful and chemical/biological category gains: AJAR +14.3 pts over PyRIT in both categories; PyRIT ahead on misinformation/disinformation (-5.9 for AJAR) and cybercrime/intrusion (-5.0 for AJAR)
Tool-access net success-set change: ActorAttack net +10 (42 gained, 32 lost); Crescendo net -26 (11 gained, 37 lost); X-Teaming net -41 (23 gained, 64 lost)

Category-level tool-access deltas: ActorAttack general harmful +28.6 pts, chemical/biological +17.9, misinformation -14.7; Crescendo general harmful -23.8, misinformation -23.5, harassment/bullying -21.1; X-Teaming general harmful -33.3, illegal acts -32.8, chemical/biological -25.0
